title: Isaiah 29
subtitle: Woe to Ariel: The Overturned Wisdom

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — “These People Honour Me with Their Lips, but Their Hearts Are Far from Me”

heb.paragraph: "Woe to you, Ariel (ʿarîʾēl), Ariel, the city where David settled!" (v.1). Ariel = Jerusalem (the altar-hearth of God). God will besiege his own city (v.3). But then enemies who attack it will vanish like a dream (vv.7–8). The people are in a spiritual stupor: "The LORD has brought over you a deep sleep" (v.10). Sealed scrolls no one can read (vv.11–12). Then the devastating quote Jesus uses most against the Pharisees: "These people come near to me with their mouth and honour me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me. Their worship of me is based on merely human rules taught by men" (v.13). Jesus quotes this in Matthew 15:8–9 and Mark 7:6–7. God will proceed to do "a wonder among these people" (v.14) — the wisdom of the wise will perish.

notes.note: "Woe to Ariel!" God besieges Jerusalem — then rescues it. The double movement: discipline and deliverance.

"The LORD has brought a deep sleep. The scroll is sealed." Spiritual blindness: the vision is right in front of them but no one can read it. The literate say "it’s sealed"; the illiterate say "I can’t read."

"These people honour me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me." Jesus’ primary weapon against the Pharisees (Matt 15:8–9; Mark 7:6–7). Lip-worship without heart-worship is Isaiah’s diagnosis of religious hypocrisy.
"The wisdom of the wise will perish, and the intelligence of the intelligent will vanish." Paul quotes this in 1 Corinthians 1:19 — God’s foolishness wiser than human wisdom.

refs.note: Jesus quotes Isaiah 29:13 against the Pharisees — honoring with lips, hearts far away.
The same quotation in Mark's context of tradition versus commandment.
Paul cites Isaiah 29:10 for Israel's hardening — spirit of stupor, eyes that cannot see.
The veil over hearts when reading Moses — Paul develops Isaiah's blindness theme.

hist.context: 'Ariel' (v.1) means 'lion of God' or 'altar hearth' — a poetic name for Jerusalem. The city that hosts God's altar will become like an altar: drenched in blood. Yet siege will be lifted 'suddenly' (v.5); the attackers will vanish like a dream. This likely refers to Sennacherib's siege in 701 BC, when the Assyrian army was mysteriously destroyed (ch.37). The 'sealed scroll' (vv.11-12) indicts spiritual blindness: the literate won't read it; the illiterate can't.

notes.note: Oswalt sees two movements: siege (vv.1-4) then deliverance (vv.5-8). The shift is abrupt — 'in an instant, suddenly.' This pattern (crisis-deliverance) shapes how readers should interpret Sennacherib's siege in chapters 36-37.
The 'lips/heart' dichotomy becomes paradigmatic for hypocrisy. Oswalt notes that religious performance without relational reality is not neutral but offensive. God's response: confounding the wise.

Childs reads 'Ariel' as emphasizing Jerusalem's cultic centrality — the altar-city. The siege threatens what makes Jerusalem Jerusalem. God's deliverance preserves not just a city but a center of worship.

Childs notes the judicial blindness theme: the people have chosen not to see; God confirms their choice. Isaiah 6's commission to harden hearts continues its outworking. The NT citations show this pattern persisting.

sections.header: Verses 15–24 — “The Deaf Will Hear; the Blind Will See”

heb.paragraph: "In that day the deaf (ḥērēsh) will hear the words of the scroll, and out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see" (v.18). Woe to those who hide their plans from God, who work in the dark and say "Who sees us?" (v.15). The potter/clay reversal: "Can the pot say to the potter, ‘You know nothing’?" (v.16) — Paul echoes this in Romans 9:20–21. But then hope: Lebanon will become a fertile field (v.17). The deaf hear, the blind see (v.18). The humble rejoice in the Holy One of Israel (v.19). The ruthless vanish (v.20). Those who erred will gain understanding (v.24). Reversal upon reversal: deafness → hearing, blindness → seeing, error → understanding.

notes.note: "Shall what is formed say to the one who formed it, ‘You did not make me’? Can the pot say of the potter, ‘You know nothing’?" Paul develops this in Romans 9:20–21. The creature cannot evaluate the Creator.

"The deaf will hear the words of the scroll; the blind will see. The humble will rejoice in the Holy One of Israel." The reversal: the sealed scroll of v.11 becomes readable. The spiritual blindness of v.10 is healed. Jesus’ ministry enacts this literally (Matt 11:5).
"Those who are wayward in spirit will gain understanding; those who complain will accept instruction." The chapter ends with learning. Error is not permanent; even the complaining come to understand.

refs.note: The deaf hearing and blind seeing — chapter 35 reverses chapter 29's judgment.
Jesus cites Isaiah's signs: blind see, deaf hear — proof of messianic identity.
The potter/clay image recurs — can the pot question the potter?
Paul develops the potter image for divine sovereignty in election.

hist.context: The 'potter and clay' saying (v.16) indicts those who think they can hide their plans from God. The political maneuvering with Egypt and Assyria proceeds as if Yahweh doesn't see or understand. The reversal promise (vv.17-24) is stunning: Lebanon becomes farmland, deaf hear, blind see, meek rejoice. The present blindness (vv.9-14) is not permanent; transformation comes 'in that day.'

notes.note: Oswalt emphasizes the absurdity: the pot calling the potter ignorant. Judah's leaders think their secret plans escape divine notice. But darkness and light are the same to God.

The transformation reverses every failure: blind see (v.18), deaf hear (v.18), meek rejoice (v.19), tyrant gone (v.20), trap-setters caught (v.21). Oswalt sees this as the pattern of messianic reversal Jesus announces.

Childs notes the chapter's structure: woe (vv.1-14), judgment on planners (vv.15-16), then hope (vv.17-24). The pattern of judgment-then-salvation recurs throughout Isaiah. Present failure does not nullify future hope.
The reference to Abraham is significant: Jacob will not be ashamed because the God who called Abraham still keeps covenant. Childs sees patriarchal promise grounding eschatological hope.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: LORD is a key figure in Woe to Ariel: The Overturned Wisdom.

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: The humble will rejoice in the Holy One of Israel…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVas when a hungry person dreams of eating, but awakens hungry still; as when a thirsty person dreams of drinking, but awakens faint and thirsty still. So will it be with the hordes of all the nations that fight against Mount Zion.ESVAs when a hungry man dreams, and behold, he is eating, and awakes with his hunger not satisfied, or as when a thirsty man dreams, and behold, he is drinking, and awakes faint, with his thirst not quenched, so shall the multitude of all the nations be that fight against Mount Zion.NIVBrought low, you will speak from the ground; your speech will mumble out of the dust. Your voice will come ghostlike from the earth; out of the dust your speech will whisper.ESVAnd you will be brought low; from the earth you shall speak, and from the dust your speech will be bowed down; your voice shall come from the ground like the voice of a ghost, and from the dust your speech shall whisper.

rec.text: ""Woe to Ariel!" God besieges Jerusalem — then rescues it. The double movement: discipline and deliverance."

rows.label: Deaf Hear, Blind See — 29:15–24
rows.text: Potter and clay (Rom 9:20). "The DEAF will hear; the BLIND will see." Error → understanding.

hebtext.note: "Woe to you, Ariel (ʿarîʾēl), Ariel, the city where David settled!" (v.1). Ariel = Jerusalem (the altar-hearth of God). God will besiege his own city (v.3). But then enemies who attack it will vanish like a dream (vv.7–8). The people are in a spiritual stupor: "The LORD has brought over you a deep sleep" (v.10). Sealed scrolls no one can read (vv.11–12). Then the devastating quote Jesus uses most against the Pharisees: "These people come near to me with their mouth and honour me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me. Their worship of me is based on merely human rules taught by men" (v.13). Jesus quotes this in Matthew 15:8–9 and Mark 7:6–7. God will proceed to do "a wonder among these people" (v.14) — the wisdom of the wise will perish.

"In that day the deaf (ḥērēsh) will hear the words of the scroll, and out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see" (v.18). Woe to those who hide their plans from God, who work in the dark and say "Who sees us?" (v.15). The potter/clay reversal: "Can the pot say to the potter, ‘You know nothing’?" (v.16) — Paul echoes this in Romans 9:20–21. But then hope: Lebanon will become a fertile field (v.17). The deaf hear, the blind see (v.18). The humble rejoice in the Holy One of Israel (v.19). The ruthless vanish (v.20). Those who erred will gain understanding (v.24). Reversal upon reversal: deafness → hearing, blindness → seeing, error → understanding.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks: Jesus quotes v.13 against the Pharisees; Paul quotes v.14 and v.16. The deaf-hear/blind-see reversal: Jesus enacts this literally. Potter/clay logic (Rom 9:20).

coaching.tip: 'These people come near to me with their mouth and honour me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me' (v. 13). Jesus quotes this verbatim in Matthew 15:8–9 against the Pharisees. Isaiah identifies a perennial religious pathology: ritual compliance masking spiritual absence. The problem isn't bad theology but disconnected theology — correct words without corresponding reality.

The reversal in verses 17–24 — deaf hearing, blind seeing, meek rejoicing — provides Jesus's evidence list when John the Baptist asks 'Are you the one?' (Matthew 11:5). Jesus doesn't argue theology; he points to Isaiah 29 being fulfilled. Isaiah wrote the diagnostic criteria for the Messiah; Jesus submitted the lab results.
